entry.title: Re: Purpose use of Offset account SAP Business One
entry.content:
Hello Nikunj,

If you're looking to understand this process within SAP B1 then we use the WIP as our offset account during production while executing below 2 transaction:

  • Issue for Production: When raw materials are issued for production, the system typically credits the inventory account and debits a work-in-progress (WIP) or production expense account.

  • Receipt from Production: When the finished goods are received, the WIP or production expense account is typically credited, and the finished goods inventory account is debited.

So, the offset account used in "Issue for Production" should indeed be cleared or balanced out by the "Receipt from Production" transaction, assuming all quantities and costs align. This also helps the business to easily track the raw material & resource in production and reflect it in their reporting as well.

Hope this would be helpful. 

Best Regards,

Kuldeep

entry.title: SAP Datasphere - Remote Table dynamic filter pushdown
entry.content:
Problem description

When integrating data via Remote Table in SAP Datasphere, filtering is limited. The limitations depend on the complexity of the SQL the DPAgent is able to push down to the source. An often seen requirement is to add a filter where the data should be filtered dynamically. This Blog shows a workaround to achieve a dynamic filtering.

For this use case, we use the ABAP Table MSEG which is not delta enabled. The MSEG table contains information about materials whenever a material movement occurs. We want to determine all materials that have a "Best-Before date" which is exactly 200 days in the future. For that, we want to add a dynamic filter on the"Best-Before date" field, which is called "VFDAT". It is being loaded into Datasphere as a String(8) in the format YYYYMMDD.

Inside the remote table itself we can only define a hard filter on a specific date. What we want instead is a dynamic filter so the integrated data changes as time goes on.

1st approach

The easiest approach is to build a view on top of the unpersisted remote table. The SQL can look like this:

 

 

select "MANDT","MBLNR","MJAHR","VFDAT" from QS4_MSEG where "VFDAT" = ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, 200)

 

 

The result set consists of 3 records. But when looking at the Remote Query of this View, the WHERE clause is not being sent to the source. The we get 3 records in the end but there are 34,516 records moving between the source system and Datasphere. So Datasphere queries the entire dataset and then filters afterwards to show the correct result because it is unable to push the WHERE clause to the source, as it is too complex.

When working with huge amounts of data, we want to limit the amount of data moving between the systems.
For that, we need to find a way to push the WHERE clause to the source.

2nd approach

We can do so by declaring variables in an SQL Script view. The SQL Script can look like this:

 

 

DECLARE myDate DATE = ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, 200);
DECLARE myDateString NVARCHAR(8) = TO_NVARCHAR(myDate, 'YYYYMMDD');
return select "MANDT","MBLNR","MJAHR","VFDAT" from QS4_MSEG where "VFDAT" = myDateString;

 

 

Instead of trying to push the complex WHERE clause, this script resolves the WHERE clause first when declaring the variable. The result set consist of the same 3 records as before, but when looking at the remote query, we can see that there WHERE clause is being pushed. It cannot visualise the WHERE clause in the remote query, so a "?" is being displayed but we can see in that only 3 records are being pulled.

Conclusion

It is apparent that the query from approach 2 is faster and leaner than the query from approach 1. It is possible that the data volume requires the leanness and pace of approach 2. This seems like a valid workaround for all use cases that depend on a dynamic filtering when integrating data via remote table.

entry.title: Nationality picklist in RCM - Is there a default list?
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Still new to SAP SF, asking for some guidance here again. 🙂

We recently realized that our Nationality picklist under the candidate profile holds so many weird values it is hard not to laugh. For example, just a few:

Bolivia (Plurinational state of)
Finn
Georgia people
Greenland people
an indian
Sweatshirt man
Lao People's Democratic Republic
Saint Martin (French part)
Rèè ? Alliance man
Swede
Thailand people?

I'm pretty sure this was customized by someone using some weird translator, or something.

My question would be if there is a default list from SAP that we can go to and revert this list back to? If so, how is that achievable? 

Thank you!

Adorján

entry.title: Announcing the Winner of "My Most Geeky Hobby” Contest
entry.content:
Announcing the Winner of "My Most Geeky Hobby” contest which ran in SAP Community – Coffee Corner - from Aug 7, 2024, at 3PM to Sept 4, 2024, at 3PM CET.

In the past, we’ve seen discussions from community members around travel, baking, favourite movies, whatever it is they love to do in their spare time. This triggered the idea to run a contest and for the past month we’ve asked you to share with us your geeky hobby photo and a fun title. We were so intrigued to see what the entries would look like. Who are our SAP Community members? Are they Star Wars fans? Gamers? Collectors? A big thank you to everyone who entered the contest! You haven’t disappointed with your entries and now it’s time to announce the winner - chosen by our SAP Community members with highest number of kudos at the time of content close (Sept 4th - 3pm CET)!

Winning Entry: From Monday Blues to Data Delights: My Geeky Hobby 🙂

Congratulations to @bhavinvyas !!

 

2nd Place Entry: Let’s Fill the Office with Action Figures!

Thank you for making us smile @Zamichiei !!

 

3rd Place Entry: My most geeky hobby? Teaching the powerful ABAP language !!

Thanks for entering @Sandra_Solis and congrats on coming third!

As the winner, @bhavinvyas will receive an SAP merchandise voucher worth 40 euros!

A big shout out to all the fun entries! All contest participants will be awarded the “Interesting Hobbyist” badge for your community profile in the coming days.
@DataTherapist | @mariaweber | @Punith_Oswal | @bhavinvyas | @Zamichiei | @Sandra_Solis | @P1_Marotkar | @Niladri_B_Nayak | @Ruthvik_Chowdary | @Himsidd | @KunalBansal | @GDR_ANZ | @gunveen | @ShaikAzmathulla | @MRobalinho | @BaerbelWinkler | @france_cabuenos_25 

Congratulations once again to @bhavinvyas on winning this contest!

SAP Community Team

entry.title: Events Project including the Events Editor Available Now !!
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the general availability of the Events Project and the Events Editor as on 4th September 2024.

 

Motivation


Events Project is the core building block of the SAP's Build portfolio for cross consumption of Events and it's artefacts. In this blog post, I will cover the general information on features of Events Project, Events Editor and more.

 

The new Events Project requires you to understand a few important terms that are listed in the following table.

 

TermsDefinition
Open API specificationIt is the standard specification for describing, producing, consuming and visualising RESTful APIs and services.
Citizen DeveloperPersona that creates application capabilities for cross consumption of API's and Events within SAP Build. 
Event DesignerIt is a persona that will use the Events Editor to define the Events Project and it's artefacts for subsequent cross-consumption.
EventA single Event that is defined by its path, payload & header parameters for cross consumption.
Event ProjectIs a collection of Event(s) that is created using an OpenAPI specification within the Events Editor by the Event Designer.
Events EditorAn intuitive Editor/tooling experience to aid the creation of Events Project. 
Actions ProjectCore Building Block of SAP Build that aids in the simplification of API consumption. 

 

What is the Events Project ?


It represents a collection of Event(s) or notification(s) with a defined signature (Path, Output, Headers) that works like a thin layer of abstraction over an Event or a Notification to simplify its consumption. The new Events Project helps to achieve the following objectives.

    1. Is built on the Events Metadata and closely in line with the Actions Metadata.
    2. Is the core of the framework and aids the design and run time consumption of an “Event”.
    3. Provides functional operability and consumption experience of Events from systems to manage your business processes.
    4. View Events and its artefact within the Event Project making it reusable across consumers.
    5. Provides publishing and discovery of an “Event” across SAP Build and more (in future).

 

What is Events Editor ?


It is developed to create Events Project while bundling its corresponding Event(s) in a simplified way. It provides the following capabilities –

    1. Discover the list of Events Package and Events Object using either SAP Business Accelerator Hub or by Uploading a custom Event Specification.
    2. View the details of Events Project and its core metadata.
    3. Select an Event to view its details such as Path, Output (Payload) and Headers.
    4. Release and Publish the Event for cross consumption within SAP Build.

Note: During creation of Events Project, the first version is always Auto Released and Published to facilitate quick consumption.

 

Discovery of Events from the Business Accelerator Hub

View Details of Events during Discovery

Event Editor

 

Usability within SBPA:

After an Events Project has been published, it could be consumed within the SBPA bound to a trigger. 


Thanks for reading, and I would really appreciate your feedback. Please feel free to leave a comment in this blog post. Please watch this space for more updates and release information on Events Project and its future upcoming scope.

References

    1. Central Blog for Actions Project can be found here.
    2. Help portal documentation on the topic Events Project and Events Editor can be found here.

entry.title: Client Handling in ABAP
entry.content:
A client is an organizational unit of an AS ABAP (Application Server ABAP, that is the application layer of an ABAP system in which ABAP programs are executed). For each logon to an AS ABAP, a client ID must be specified. A logon can happen in a quite classical way using SAP Logon and SAP GUI, via the RFC interface and nowadays most prominently using Web UIs that are handled on AS ABAP by handler classes, called from ICF (Internet Connection Framework). For all of these logons, a client ID is requested with which a logon to a specific client takes place.  Each user session is thus governed by its current client whose client ID is set during logon.

On the database, application tables have a client column as their first key field. Any database table that has a first column of built-in ABAP Dictionary type CLNT is client-dependent. System tables without a client column are client-independent. Other data sources, such as CDS views, that are based on tables with a client column are also client-dependent. The client column contains client IDs. Each client ID uniquely assigns the data in the other columns to a client. 

As a rule, ABAP application programs executed in a user session should access only data of the current client and no data of other clients (no cross-client access). This is called client isolation. Client isolation is strongly recommended in the ABAP language version Standard ABAP and must be enforced in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development.

The following sections show how client isolation is achieved and can be enforced for different data sources and different ways of accessing them.

The Good

In ABAP, the best you can do is to stay in the ABAP comfort zone and let the ABAP runtime framework do as much as possible for you. Regarding client isolation, ABAP supports you with an implicit client handling in the following cases:

ABAP SQL

When accessing client-dependent data sources with a client column with ABAP SQL, an implicit client handling takes place. By default, you cannot specify conditions for the client column of a client-dependent data source explicitly. Instead, the SQL statements passed to the database by the ABAP SQL interface contain implicit WHERE and ON conditions for the client column that select the data of the current client only. This default behavior cannot be changed in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development. The following code snippet exemplifies this behavior:

 

SELECT * FROM scarr
         "WHERE mandt = '...'           "Syntax error
         INTO TABLE (result).

SELECT * FROM scarr USING CLIENT '100'  "Forbidden in ABAP Cloud
         "WHERE mandt = '...'           "Syntax error
         INTO TABLE @result. 

SELECT * FROM scarr CLIENT SPECIFIED    "Obsolete:
         WHERE mandt = '200'            "Syntax warning in Standard ABAP
         INTO TABLE @result.            "Syntax error   in ABAP Cloud

 

  • Only the first SELECT statement is possible in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development.  Implicit client handling takes place with the default value for the client ID. An explicit WHERE condition for the client column of the client-dependent database table SCARR is forbidden (in ABAP Cloud as well as in classic ABAP).
  • The second SELECT statement is only possible in ABAP language version Standard ABAP. It switches the client ID used for implicit client handling from the current client to the client specified behind USING CLIENT. As this violates client isolation, the addition USING CLIENT is forbidden in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development.
  • The third SELECT statement uses the obsolete addition CLIENT SPECIFIED. This addition disables the implicit client handling. The client column loses its special meaning and is handled like any other key field. In order to select the data of a specific client, the client column must be used in a WHERE condition explicitly. If this is not done, the data of all clients are selected. In order to select the current client, the client column can be compared to the system field sy-mandt, whose nominal value is the client ID of the current client. The addition CLIENT SPECIFIED is forbidden in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development and leads to a syntax warning in Standard ABAP.

The following SQL trace shows the native HANA SELECT statements that are passed by the ABAP SQL interface to the SAP HANA database for the ABAP SQL statements:

 

 

In the first two statements, the WHERE conditions are introduced by ABAP SQL's implicit client handling. The first statement uses the default current client, the second statement uses the client specified behind USING CLIENT. In the third statement the WHERE condition comes from the explicit WHERE condition in ABAP SQL, where implicit client handling is switched off.

To summarize: The default behavior of ABAP SQL's implicit client handling fully supports client isolation. This behavior cannot be changed in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development. Using ABAP SQL in ABAP Cloud never violates client isolation.

ABAP CDS View Entities

The client-dependency of ABAP CDS view entities (as well as of ABAP CDS hierarchies) is defined by the data sources used by the CDS entity. If one of the data sources used in the entity is client-dependent, the entity is client-dependent. If none of the data sources used in the entity is client-dependent, the entity is client-independent.

The client handling of ABAP CDS view entities (as well as of ABAP CDS hierarchies) is done by implicitly restricting the result set to the client ID that is contained in the HANA session variable CDS_CLIENT. This can be seen from the following example of a simple CDS view entity:

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
define view entity DEMO_CDS_SCARR_ENTITY
  as select from scarr
{
  key scarr.carrid,
  key scarr.carrname,
      scarr.currcode,
      scarr.url
}

 

The implementation of the SQL view on the HANA database can be displayed in ADT:

 

 

 

 

The implicit client handling is achieved by the generated WHERE condition:

... WHERE "SCARR"."MANDT" = SESSION_CONTEXT( 'CDS_CLIENT' )

The client column is compared with the result of the built-in HANA function SESSION_CONTEXT that reads the contents of the session variable CDS_CLIENT. The procedure using an implicit WHERE condition is the same as for ABAP SQL. As always, the difference between ABAP CDS and ABAP SQL is that CDS entities are instantiated as artifacts in the database, whereas an ABAP SQL statement is passed to the database for direct execution only. Note that from ABAP's perspective, the CDS view entity doesn't have a client column at all.

From the above it is clear that client isolation depends on how the HANA session variable CDS_CLIENT is set. By default, CDS_CLIENT contains the client ID of the current client and thus is the same as the nominal value of the system field sy-mandt in ABAP programs. There is no way of changing the value of CDS_CLIENT from ABAP code (including AMDP) except the USING CLIENT addition of ABAP SQL.

After executing the following code snippet:

 

SELECT * FROM demo_cds_scarr_entity USING CLIENT '200'
         INTO TABLE (result).

 

You find the following statement in the SQL trace in front of the SELECT statement:

 

This statement switches the implicit client handling of the ABAP CDS view from the current client to an explicitly specified client. Behind the SELECT statement, the same statement is used to switch the session variable back to the current client. As for accessing database tables, the addition USING CLIENT violates client isolation. Buts as we know, it is forbidden in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development.

To summarize: The default behavior of ABAP CDS view entities when accessed with ABAP SQL (allowed in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development) never violates client isolation.

The Bad

When leaving the comfort zone of ABAP and ABAP SQL by using Native SQL, you must take care of client isolation yourself.

Native SQL

Native SQL consists of HANA-specific SQL statements that you can specify in an ABAP program and that are passed unchanged directly to the HANA database using the Native SQL interface of the AS ABAP. Native SQL statements can be specified statically between the ABAP statements EXEC SQL, ENDEXEC and dynamically with the classes of ADBC (ABAP Database Connectivity). The main task of the Native SQL interface is the mapping of ABAP data to database data and vice versa, but there is no manipulation of the SQL statement itself and thus, there is no implicit client handling.

When accessing client-dependent database tables, you must provide appropriate WHERE conditions in order to achieve client isolation, as in the following examples:

EXEC SQL:

 

EXEC SQL.
  OPEN dbcur FOR
    SELECT carrid, carrname
           FROM scarr
           WHERE mandt  = :sy-mandt
ENDEXEC.

 

ADBC:

 

  FINAL(result) = NEW cl_sql_statement( )->execute_query(
    `SELECT CARRID, CARRNAME ` &&
    `FROM SCARR ` &&
    `WHERE MANDT  = ` && `'` && sy-mandt && `'` ).

 

When accessing client-dependent CDS entities, you must avoid setting the HANA session variable CDS_CLIENT to another value than the client ID of the current client.

To summarize:  With Native SQL, client isolation can be achieved but not controlled and therefore Native SQL is not allowed in the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development.

The Others

With AMDP (ABAP Managed Database Procedures) we also leave the comfort zone of ABAP SQL and ABAP CDS. 

AMDP

AMDP enables implementing HANA database procedures and HANA database functions written in HANA SQLScript in so-called AMDP methods of AMDP classes. AMDP methods can be either called from other ABAP code or can be wrapped by CDS table functions and thus become available as data sources for ABAP CDS queries and ABAP SQL queries. While the AMDP framework manages the instantiation, handling and calling of the database procedures on the SAP HANA database, the implementation is in fact pure native SQL and cannot be controlled. But since there are good reasons for using AMDP (mainly for accessing functionality that is not yet wrapped by ABAP CDS DQL or ABAP SQL DQL), AMDP is released for ABAP Cloud and there must be a way to achieve client isolation.

Before ABAP Cloud, client handling was done in AMDP in the same way as for Native SQL described above: As a rule, you had to take care of client isolation yourself. It was recommended to pass the current client ID as a parameter to an AMDP method and to use it in WHERE conditions in the SQLScript method implementation. Furthermore, an AMDP method could be declared with the following AMDP options:

METHODS meth ... AMDP OPTIONS CDS SESSION CLIENT CURRENT|clnt

These additions set the HANA session variable CDS_CLIENT when the AMDP method is called from ABAP. CURRENT sets the session variable to the client ID of the current client and when a variable clnt is specified its value is used. Inside the implementation, the session variable can determine the client handling of the database artifacts of CDS entities accessed there (see above) or it can be used explicitly in WHERE conditions.

Unfortunately, both passing the current client as a parameter as well as using the AMDP option CDS SESSION CLIENT CURRENT are both not enough to achieve client isolation. There is no control over whether the passed client ID or the session variable are used appropriately in the implementation to restrict access to the current client. In other words: An AMDP method whose client handling is defined by using no AMDP option for the client handling, or by using the AMDP option CDS SESSION CLIENT CURRENT, is not client-safe. A development object that accesses client-dependent SQL data sources is client-safe if it can access data of one client only. In order to achieve client isolation for AMDP, an AMDP method must be made client-safe.

To achieve client safety for AMDP methods, new AMDP options have been introduced:

ABAP release 7.93/7.58 (2308/2023)

METHODS meth ... AMDP OPTIONS CLIENT INDEPENDENT

The AMDP option CLIENT INDEPENDENT declares an AMDP method as client-independent. Only client-independent database objects can be accessed and specified after its USING addition. The AMDP method is client-safe since no client-dependent data sources can be accessed.

ABAP release 7.96/8.16 (2405/2025)

METHODS meth ... AMDP OPTIONS CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT

The AMDP option CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT declares an AMDP method as client-dependent. As the former CDS SESSION CLIENT CURRENT, it also sets the HANA session variable CDS_CLIENT to the client ID of the current client. But in contrast to the former option CDS SESSION CLIENT CURRENT, it also restricts the USING list of the AMDP method to the following client-safe objects and thus makes the AMDP method itself client-safe:

  • Client-dependent CDS view entities filtered by the HANA session variable CDS_CLIENT
  • Client-dependent CDS table functions that are client-safe (see below).
  • Client-independent CDS objects
  • Client-dependent or client-independent AMDP methods with the AMDP option CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT or CLIENT INDEPENDENT

Note that no direct access to client-dependent database tables is permitted in client-safe AMDP methods. In ABAP Cloud, where client isolation is a must, only client-safe AMDP methods are permitted. AMDP methods that have ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development, or are released as APIs for ABAP Cloud, must have one of the AMDP options CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT or CLIENT INDEPENDENT and their USING lists are respectively restricted. AMDP methods that are not client-safe lead to syntax warnings in ABAP Cloud that will become syntax errors in the future.

The following is an example of a client-dependent AMDP method that is client-safe.

 

CLASS ...

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_amdp_marker_hdb .

    TYPES:
      t_carriers TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF demo_cds_scarr_entity
                 WITH EMPTY KEY .

    METHODS get_scarr
      AMDP OPTIONS
      READ-ONLY
      CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT
      EXPORTING
        VALUE(carriers) TYPE t_carriers
      RAISING
        cx_amdp_error .

ENDCLASS.

CLASS ... IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get_scarr BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
                        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
                        USING demo_cds_scarr_entity.
      carriers  = select carrid, carrname, currcode, url
                         from demo_cds_scarr_entity;
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Note, that no parameter for passing the client ID is needed anymore. When refactoring existing AMDP methods to client-safe AMDP methods you can remove such a parameter. If you must keep it for compatibility reasons, be aware that the result is empty for all passed clients other than the current client, if the parameter is used in WHERE conditions, since the results of the allowed data sources are implicitly restricted to the current client.

To summarize: For AMDP methods, special AMDP options CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT or CLIENT INDEPENDENT must be applied in order to achieve client isolation by restricting the USING list. This is recommended in classic ABAP and mandatory in ABAP Cloud.

CDS Table Functions

You may have noticed that the above discussion is valid for standalone AMDP methods that can be called from ABAP code only. For AMDP methods implementing ABAP CDS table functions, none of the above AMDP options are allowed. Instead, such AMDP methods are declared with:

CLASS-METHODS meth FOR TABLE FUNCTION cds_tabfunc.

In that case, the client handling is determined by the CDS table function. 

From ABAP release 9.13/8.16 (2411/2025) and on, there is a new CDS annotation available for CDS table functions:

@ClientHandling.clientSafe

This annotation declares a CDS table function explicitly as client-safe and the following restrictions apply:

  • All used objects must be client-safe.
  • The table function must have one of the annotations
    • @ClientHandling.type with the value #CLIENT_DEPENDENT together with @ClientHandling.algorithm with the value #SESSION_VARIABLE.
    • @ClientHandling.type with the value #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT.
  • The table function must not have a parameter of type CLNT. The annotation @Environment.systemField with the enumerated value #CLIENT cannot be used for a parameter.

That means that a client-safe table function is either client-dependent, and the client handling is done using the HANA session variable CDS_CLIENT, or it is client independent. The AMDP method that implements a client-safe table function is then implicitly defined as with the AMDP option CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT or CLIENT INDEPENDENT respectively.

The following is an example of a client-safe CDS table function:

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_ALLOWED
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_DEPENDENT
@ClientHandling.algorithm: #SESSION_VARIABLE
@ClientHandling.clientSafe: true
define table function DEMO_CDS_TABFUNC_SESS_VAR_CS
returns
{
  client   :s_mandt;
  carrid   :s_carrid;
  carrname :s_carrname;
}
implemented by method
  cl_demo_cds_tabfunc_sess_vr_cs=>get;

 

The implementing AMDP class looks as follows:

 

CLASS cl_demo_cds_tabfunc_sess_vr_cs DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_amdp_marker_hdb.
    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF scarr,
        client TYPE scarr-mandt,
        cyrrid TYPE scarr-carrid,
      END OF scarr,
      scarr_tab TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF scarr
                     WITH EMPTY KEY.
    CLASS-METHODS get
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION demo_cds_tabfunc_sess_var_cs.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS CL_DEMO_CDS_TABFUNC_SESS_VR_CS IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get
         BY DATABASE FUNCTION FOR HDB
         LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
         OPTIONS READ-ONLY
         USING demo_cds_scarr_entity.
    RETURN select sc.mandt as client, sc.carrid, sc.carrname
                  from demo_cds_scarr_entity as sc
                  ORDER BY sc.carrid;
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

The AMDP method GET is implicitly client-safe. For its USING list, the same restrictions apply as if the AMDP option CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT is used explicitly.

For a CDS table function that has the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development or one that is released as an API, the annotation @ClientHandling.clientSafe is set implicitly. There are also the earlier options for client handling of CDS table functions, which implicitly set the corresponding AMDP options for the respective AMDP methods, but this will not be discussed further here.

To summarize: AMDP methods that implement client-safe CDS table functions implicitly work as with the AMDP options CDS SESSION CLIENT DEPENDENT or CLIENT INDEPENDENT and are client-safe. Only client-safe CDS table functions are allowed in ABAP Cloud.

entry.title: Default Planned work time in operation for PM order
entry.content:
My requirement is to how populate default Planned work time, here in the PM order operation:

entry.title: Comparing SAP Analytics Cloud Planning Security vs. OneStream Security: A Comprehensive Analysis
entry.content:
In the modern landscape of enterprise performance management (EPM) and analytics, ensuring robust security is paramount. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Planning and OneStream are two leading platforms offering powerful tools for planning, forecasting, and financial analysis. However, when it comes to security, how do these two giants compare? This blog will delve into a detailed comparison of the security features of SAP Analytics Cloud Planning and OneStream, providing insights for businesses aiming to make an informed decision.

 

 

 

 

Overview of SAP Analytics Cloud Planning Security

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is a comprehensive solution that combines business intelligence, planning, and predictive analytics in a single cloud environment. Security in SAC Planning is deeply integrated into the platform, ensuring data protection, user access control, and compliance with global security standards.

  1. Data Encryption

    • At Rest: SAC employs robust encryption mechanisms for data at rest, using AES-256 encryption, which is a widely recognized industry standard.
    • In Transit: All data transmitted between the client and server is encrypted using TLS (Transport Layer Security) protocols, ensuring secure communication.

  2. User Authentication and Access Control

    • Single Sign-On (SSO): SAC supports SSO through SAML 2.0, allowing users to access the platform seamlessly while ensuring secure authentication.
    • Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): SAC offers detailed role-based access controls, enabling administrators to define permissions based on user roles, ensuring that sensitive data is only accessible to authorized personnel.

  3. Data Privacy and Compliance

    • GDPR Compliance: SAC is compliant with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), providing tools for data anonymization and user consent management.
    • Data Centers: SAC operates on a network of secure data centers worldwide, ensuring data residency and compliance with regional regulations.

  4. Audit and Monitoring

    • SAC provides comprehensive audit logs that track user activities, including data access and modifications. This feature is critical for maintaining transparency and ensuring accountability within the platform.

Overview of OneStream Security

OneStream, a unified platform for financial planning and consolidation, also offers a strong security framework designed to protect sensitive financial data and ensure compliance.

  1. Data Encryption

    • At Rest: OneStream uses encryption techniques like AES-256 to protect data stored within the platform.
    • In Transit: Similar to SAC, OneStream ensures data security during transmission through TLS protocols.

  2. User Authentication and Access Control

    • SSO and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): OneStream supports SSO and also offers MFA options, adding an extra layer of security to the authentication process.
    • Security Profiles: OneStream’s security model includes detailed security profiles that control user access at various levels, including application, dimension, and data level.

  3. Data Privacy and Compliance

    • Regulatory Compliance: OneStream is compliant with various global regulations, including GDPR, ensuring that the platform meets stringent data protection standards.
    • Security Certifications: OneStream holds certifications like SOC 2 Type II, demonstrating its commitment to maintaining a secure environment.

  4. Audit and Monitoring

    • OneStream offers extensive logging and monitoring capabilities, allowing organizations to track changes, access history, and maintain an audit trail for compliance purposes.

 

SAC Application Security

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) securities revolve around user management, roles, and teams. Users are assigned roles that determine their access to data and functionalities. Roles are typically categorized by business needs and linked to specific permissions. SAC also emphasizes organizing content within secure folders and projects to maintain control over data access. Best practices include avoiding default folders, structuring content based on projects, and regularly reviewing user roles to ensure they align with organizational policies.

OneStream Application Security

OneStream security best practices highlight the importance of a layered security approach. It is advised to set up detailed security profiles to control user access at different levels, including application, dimension, and data. Best practices include regularly reviewing and updating security profiles, using Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for enhanced protection, and carefully managing access permissions based on user roles and responsibilities. Maintaining securities thorough audit logs and monitoring user activities are crucial for ensuring compliance and security.

 

Conclusion

Choosing between SAP Analytics Cloud Planning and OneStream requires careful consideration of your organization’s security needs. Both platforms offer strong security measures, but differences in authentication options, compliance certifications, and user access management may sway your decision. Ultimately, your choice should align with your company’s specific security requirements and regulatory obligations.

Whether you prioritize the comprehensive role-based controls of SAP Analytics Cloud or the enhanced authentication features of OneStream, both platforms are well-equipped to protect your critical business data.

entry.title: Re: How to retrieve daily work calendar from EC Timesheet...
entry.content:
Hi, 

not quite clear what you want to achieve. We have currently only internal apis that query the planned working time, no public available APIs. However, you can report on the planned working time if this helps - check my last blog where I explained this.

But if you just need the planned working time for whatever reasons in the integration center there are multiple options:

1) Make this employees to negative time recorders. If they don´t record anything on a day the planned working time is used as recorded working time. And you can create then with 1 simple time valuation a time type group set as pay type. This is then stored on the database and you have the data per any day in the integration center as time valuation result.

2) You can also fill a time container. This should have the same effect. But probably most easiest is Option 1

best regards

Volker

entry.title: Four questions to ask when adding CDC, CDP and Emarsys to your marketing toolset
entry.content:
Coming off another disappointing loss to the Kansas City Chiefs in the NFL Super Bowl LVIII earlier this year, San Francisco 49ers football head coach Kyle Shanahan offered his perspective on the team’s mentality going into the offseason:

You look at (every option) every single year and you try to become the best team possible with the situation that you're in. You don't just say, 'Hey, we're upgrading here this year.' You've got to make sure that's available. And if it's not, then you get stronger in a different area. There's lots of ways you can win. You just have to keep trying to find that way.

This is a puzzle that teams across all professional sports leagues are trying to solve as they return from an offseason of free agent signings, trades and transactions. How will new personnel fit into our organization and how do we optimize their strengths? What kind of process and platform changes will new leadership and their processes bring? How do these changes move the needle and bring us closer to our season goals?

One well-known NHL sports team is asking these questions not only about their team but also recent changes to their marketing technology.

Over the past three summers, this customer took steps to optimize their business’ approach to customer marketing with the addition of three new SAP systems–Customer Data Cloud (CDC), Customer Data Platform (CDP) and Emarsys–and have been monitoring performance of select email campaigns ever since. These solutions replaced a longtime player in their CX architecture–SAP Marketing Cloud (SMC)–which the customer had been using for years create segments of users across multiple sources and market to them with personalized email communications.

A system overhaul like this requires not only business investment in the new technology, but also team training, solution alignment and periodic assessment. From discovery through launch, the customer kept in mind the following questions–ones that any company should consider when planning a shift from a single platform to a multi-cloud solution.

What value do we get from the trade?

While SMC helped the team with email marketing needs, members were never able to take full advantage of the platform due, in part, to platform limitations. With the SAP acquisition of CDC (formerly “Gigya”) in late 2017 and Emarsys in 2020, along with the release of CDP in 2020, the customer saw an opportunity to improve their marketing solution and leverage strengths from each platform:

  • CDC – for capturing user marketing consent, newsletter opt-ins and customer data through Screen-Sets and Hosted Pages.
  • CDP – for handling complex merging, segmentation and orchestration with systems based on data privacy permissions.
  • Emarsys – for marketing automation, improved workflows and customer experience.

How do new players fit within the current system?

For the initial two program phases, the customer focused on replacing SMC with a core CX framework that leverages CDC and DSP as data sources, paving the way for future integrations. 

CX Marketing Architecture including CDC, CDP and Emarsys

 By shifting from SMC to a multi-cloud solution, the customer can get the most value out of each system while not being restricted to just one platform. The flexibility and agility gained through this approach will be key as the customer looks to connect to other data sources, both from SAP and outside of SAP.

How well does each player know their role?

With multiple solutions, however, comes a new question around overlapping features and functions.

Just like in any team with talented players, there are bound to be some systems with overlapping capabilities. There isn’t one solution that fits all, so it’s important to invest time to clearly define the roles of each application and decide which platform is best suited for handling day-to-day tasks.

Take, for example, segmentation. Both CDP and Emarsys platforms have tools to segment users, so which system should customers use to build the segment?

For this customer, the direction largely depended on answers to a few questions:

  • What are the business use cases / campaigns that we are looking to launch in this phase?
  • What data needs to be available in each system to carry out the campaigns?
  • What system would be easier for a non-technical marketing team to use and maintain?

In the end, the solution that the customer landed on involved a combination of the two platforms:

  • Emarsys for non-complex segmentation needs (e.g. subscribed to a newsletter or are part of a specific tier). This requires that any permanent, re-usable contact information from CDP is synced with Emarsys.
  • CDP for more advanced field calculations and segmentation (e.g. users who purchased tickets to X game) and send trigger events to Emarsys when email (e.g. pre-event, post-event) should be sent to the user.

How are we going to develop our players and team?

The goal for the development staffing for any professional sports team is to try and turn players into serviceable assets. The same can be said for new marketing technologies.

The customer is already looking forward to the next integrations that require more advanced capabilities of CDC, CDP and Emarsys and expand the dataset coming from DSP. With a myriad of other data sources available in their network, from parking reservations and restaurant purchases to storefront and arena (Wi-Fi) logins, there are many ways to further develop the solution and personalize customer communication, which in turn breeds loyal customers.

It won’t happen overnight, of course. Developing a multi-cloud marketing solution requires thoughtful planning, iterative updates, and commitment. While this customer’s off-season marketing technology changes marked a big step in the right direction, there is still plenty of work ahead to realize their full marketing vision.

entry.title: How to change the destination stock type of a HU - Usage of BAdI /SCWM/EX_CORE_GM_CAT
entry.content:
Requirement:During internal transfer posting of a Full HU (/SCWM/ADHU) or a Product transfer( /SCWM/ADPROD) using Fiori or RF transaction, there shall be a capability of switching the destination stock type from its source one. If source HU has stock type has F2, the destination stock type of HU should be Q4 or B6. This is primarily used in industry where HU or partial HU stock will undergo Tempering or Thawing process( A kind of Quarantine for specific Hour),a process of improving the state or characteristics  of the product. After certain hours when tempering time is over, the Stock type should be automatically changed from QQ to FF in the same storage type via background job reading HU custom able

Problem Addressed/ Standard Scenario: There is no standard functionality available in SAP which allows to flip the destination HU stock type on the fly when it is internally moved from one storage type A to destination Storage type B. One way is to hard code the 'Non Location Independent Stock Type'' QQ or BB in the destination storage type. However, the major pitfall is the storage type will never allow the posting change from QQ or BB to FF in Standard.

Solution: This blog will showcase a solution how to flip the stock type even hard coding the the 'Non Location Independent Stock Type'' QQ or BB to F2 in config .Also, there is no Badi available in EWM landscape which allows to change destination stock type at Warehouse task level. In the past there was BADI /SCWM/EX_WHO_DSTGRP which allowed to change everything on WT level, this door is now closed unfortunately. 

The above issue is raised in customer connection as well.

Improvement Request Details - Customer Influence (sap.com)

However, there is workaround of flipping the stock type using Badi /SCWM/EX_CORE_GM_CAT.  This BAdI Allows  to determine the destination stock type in the case of automatic posting change upon warehouse task confirmation.

The current logic of stock type change is based on certain condition when a HU is internally moved from Storage type A to Storage Type B( QQ Hard code), system will flip the stock type from FF to QQ which is all standard. However after quarantine hour is over, the posting change from Monitor, /SCWM/POST, using  program /SCWM/R_STOCK_TYPE_CHANGE or using FM /SCWM/STOCK_CHANGE  allows to change the stock type from QQ to FF using Badi BAdI /SCWM/EX_CORE_GM_CAT. 

In Standard, once storage type stock category is hard coded, it never allows to switch from QQ to FF or BB. System will always give an error message stock type change is not allowed. Hence, the above BAdI helps to ve multiple stock type even storage type allows single Stock category.

Storage type Config: The storage type has stock category as 'QQ'. Here QQ stock category is without QM. Customer can also chose BB if HU needs some rest time before it is switched back to FF.

In nutshell, the solution revolves into 2 aspects.

  1. Movement of HU from X to Y storage type and posting change from FF to QQ via Storage type config
  2. Posting change of HU from QQ to FF, the destination stock type change upon WT confirmation along with BAdI /SCWM/EX_CORE_GM_CAT to skip hard coded configuration.

  1. Perform a internal Transfer of an HU

The stock type changes to Q4 based on standard configuration.

Perform the stock type change from Q4 to F2 via Warehouse Monitor considering tempering time is over.

 

Post BAdI implementation, system would allow to flip the stock type disregarding storage type configuration.

Badi details:

To allow the scope of this BAdI only for tempering storage type, we have added an external switch so to influence the stock type change specific for tempering storage type . For other storage type, the logic will not influence and execute as per standard logic.

Below is the switch created which is public attribute and has been utilized externally to set 'X'.

Thanks for reading.

The blog has been co-authored by @prem_swaroop from SAP.

entry.title: Re: #COE #BA01 Schedule Post Closing and Opening Balances...
entry.content:
Hola P_Quiroz_23 

According to the case description, I think you are talking about the Balance Carryforward job automatically. If this is the case, the balance carryforward job is scheduled for approx. Two weeks before the fiscal year closes. This job, which is not visible to the users, is executed every night after midnight. This job checks, for each company code and ledger combination, whether a balance carryforward job should be scheduled for this company code and ledger combination.


However, it is possible to run the job from the Schedule General ledger job app manually, but you would wait until all periods of the previous fiscal year are closed to avoid such a situation. This means that a manual balance carryforward is only possible after the start of the next fiscal year. And every journal entry, which was already handled by the automatic balance carryforward, is ignored. The system won’t duplicate postings.
The Balance Carry Forward Status app provides the planned, in-process, or completed status.

More details in the following links:

- 3014633 - Technical Background to Automatic Scheduling of Balance Carryforward Runs

- SAP Help Portal Balance Carryforward

Best regards

Edna

entry.title: Re: Duplicate Inspection lot creation on Sales Return Ins...
entry.content:
Is it this one?

3271931 - Inspection lot with origin 17 is created despite QM is deactivated for movement type in OMJJ

Resolution

In order to restrict inspection lot creation in EWM environment, following possibilities can be considered:

A) Use EWM inspection rule to restrict the lot creation for certain document type, item type or other properties. In decentralized EWM landscape, inspection rules are used by default. In embedded EWM landscape, the setting to use inspection rules can be reached at following customizing: transaction SPRO ---> SCM Extended Warehouse Management ---> Extended Warehouse Management ---> Cross-Process Settings ---> Quality Management ---> Basic and Integration ---> Define and Activate Warehouse-Dependent IOTs.

Inspection rules can be maintained in transaction /SCWM/QRSETUP.

B) Check if BAdI /SCWM/EX_QM_INSP_RELEVANCE (method: INSPECTION_RELEVANCE_SET) can be used to prevent inspection lot creation according to the business requirement. This BAdI is available for both decentralized and embedded landscape.

entry.title: Re: How to retrieve daily work calendar from EC Timesheet...
entry.content: Hi Volker, You have solved our problem in one go. "negative time recorders. If they don´t record anything on a day the planned working time is used as recorded working time" - we were not aware of this, but this is the key to the answer. Once we changed it to negative, the employee's standard working hours from the Work Schedule were instantly available in the employeeTime OData object. Very much appreciated - thank you. Kind regards / LG, Alan

entry.title: Re: TS-Based Supply Optimizer finishes before Maximum Sol...
entry.content:
Hi Vincent,

If the optimizer finished before your Maximum Solving Time, it means the optimizer found an optimal solution within 2,500 seconds. This is true for any discrete and linear run: if the optimizer finds the optimal solution (i.e. solution gap < 10e-6), it will stop before reaching the Max Solving Time.

You might want to test increasing your product decomposition's window size because it might be too small and the optimizer is solving it too fast.

Best regards,
Matheus

entry.title: OpenDocument String to Stored Procedure Universe
entry.content:
To invoke reports from our application we use the OpenDocument interface. Some report parameters allow for multiple selections. In the OpenDocument url, multiple values for a parameter are specified by separating each value with a semicolon, and the parameter is prefixed with lsM (to specify that the parameter supports multiple values). The problem occurs with some of our WebI reports that use a Stored Procedure universe (sp_unv). Our stored procedures use a varchar stored procedure parameter to receive the multiple values, a single string that contains the different values separated by semicolons. However, it seems SAP BO 4.3 is unable to map the multiple values to a stored procedure parameter of nvarchar type. 

While we can change the parameter prefix to lsS, and pass all the values to the stored procedure as a single string, the problem is that we use the same variable in other native WebI queries too. We can modify our application code to specify variables that support multiple values twice, and append "AsStr" to the parameter name. Then modify the universe to map stored procedures parameters to the "AsStr" prompt variable. For example, if ProviderID supports multiple values, we can append two parameters to the OpenDocument url, one called lsMProviderID=1;2;3 and another one called lsSProviderIDAsStr=1;2;3. The stored procedure parameter in the stored proc universe would then be changed from ProviderID to ProviderIDAsStr.

We will contact SAP support to see if there is another way to handle this, maybe there is a backwards compatible setting that would behave the same as SAP 4.2, and pass multiple values to the stored procedure as a string (nvarchar)? We did not seem to have this issue with version 4.2. By passing the values separated by ";" it would pass the list to one query, and send it as a string to the stored procedure universe.

entry.title: Top Reasons to Attend SAP TechEd 2024
entry.content:
SAP TechEd is a high-energy, interactive virtual event experience, which is accessible to all. With a jam-packed agenda and some of the top tech topics AND speakers – there really are very few reasons why NOT to attend! The overall SAP TechEd program journey provides many opportunities to the individual attendee and by browsing the Session Catalog in advance, you have time – not only to choose your sessions of interest carefully – but also to prepare your questions in advance for the interactive sessions!

One of our top leaders and contributors across SAP Community and ecosystem is SAP Champion, Kartik Dua. Learn from Kartik how to supercharge your career at SAP TechEd: boost your skills, strengthen your role, and explore new horizons.

Kartik Dua (USA) | SAP Champion since 2019 | Company: Xylem | Number of SAP TechEd events attended: 3.
In the ever-evolving world of technology, staying ahead of the curve is essential for any tech professional. Whether you're an SAP expert, developer, architect, or just beginning your journey in tech, SAP TechEd offers a unique opportunity to supercharge your career. Here's how SAP TechEd can help you advance, become more effective in your role, and expand your technical education into exciting new fields like AI.

What You Will Learn to Advance Your Career

SAP TechEd is more than just a tech conference; it's a career-defining experience. At this event, you'll have access to the latest knowledge and trends in enterprise technology. From hands-on workshops and expert-led sessions to deep dives into SAP's innovative solutions, you'll acquire valuable insights that can help you stay competitive in the fast-paced tech landscape.

You'll learn about emerging technologies such as AI, machine learning, cloud computing, and data management that are reshaping industries. By mastering these skills, you'll position yourself as an innovator within your organization, opening doors to new opportunities and career growth.

Sharpen Your Skills for Success in Your Role

Beyond the big-picture career benefits, SAP TechEd provides highly practical learning experiences that will enhance your day-to-day performance. Through technical workshops, real-world case studies, and in-depth training, you’ll gain hands-on expertise to improve your job effectiveness.

You'll walk away with practical solutions to optimize your use of SAP technology, streamline processes, and overcome complex challenges within your organization. This knowledge will not only make you more competent but also more confident in your ability to drive positive outcomes in your role.

Expand Your Technical Education into new Fields Like AI

One of the most exciting aspects of SAP TechEd is the opportunity to broaden your technical skillset. In today's digital economy, cross-functional expertise is increasingly important. At SAP TechEd, you can explore new fields like artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, and automation—technologies that are revolutionizing the business world.

By expanding your knowledge in these areas, you'll not only enhance your personal skill set but also help future-proof your career. AI and related technologies are becoming central to how organizations operate, and having expertise in these fields can make you indispensable in your company’s digital journey.

Why SAP TechEd Should Be Your Next Stop

SAP TechEd is a unique event where you’ll grow not just as a professional but also as a thought leader. You’ll have the chance to connect with like-minded peers, industry pioneers, and SAP experts who are pushing the boundaries of enterprise technology. The insights and skills you’ll gain here will have a lasting impact on your career trajectory.

Whether your goal is to climb the ranks, sharpen your current skills, or explore the future of technology, SAP TechEd offers the knowledge and network to help you succeed. Make SAP TechEd your next move and get ready to shape the future of your career in tech.

Kartik Dua (SAP Champion)

More Reasons to Attend

Kunal Bansal (India) | SAP Champion since 2023 | Company: IBM India | Number of TechEd events attended: 2.
- Connect with SAP Mentors, SAP Champions, experts, developers, and thought leaders to build connections.
- Discover the latest innovations driving the digital transformation, including SAP Joule, AI-assisted integrations, and SAP BTP.

Tammy Powlas (USA) | SAP Mentor since 2010 | Company: Fairfax Water| Number of SAP TechEd events attended: 14.
- See how SAP is executing what was discussed at Sapphire and help plan for 2025.  “It’s not about marketing, but what the product can do”.
- It’s free, no travel.

Gregor Wolf (Germany) | SAP Mentor since 2007 | Company: Computerservice Wolf | Number of SAP TechEd events attended: Way too many to count!
- Dive deep with the updated hands-on tutorials.
- Use the live chat to address questions directly to SAP product owners, product management or developers.

Ankur Godre (UK) | SAP Mentor since 2015 | Company: LTIMindtree | Number of SAP TechEd events attended: 3.
- Get insights into what the future holds:
Keep yourselves abreast of the latest and greatest in the SAP world (Joule, Business AI and more) and get deep insights for planning your organization’s tech strategy with the unmissable keynotes providing insights into what's coming in the future, the technology trends, product launches, key announcements and SAP's strategic direction.
- Learning, Certification and Career advancement:
No tech event is of value if you are not learning something new. Learn through several hands-on practical and tailored sessions of your choice, get certified onsite with a bonus advantage of discounted price, and use the knowledge and certification gained to advance your career further.
- Networking with Industry peers/experts and inspiration from success stories:
Build productive relationships & share experiences with professionals from varied industries and organizations, whether you are a developer, architect, business leader or partner. Finally, there is no better validation than to hear directly from customers who have successfully implemented different SAP solutions to solve their business problems. Get inspiration and ideas to write your own success story!

With all these top reasons, we know you won’t want to miss out on this great opportunity to attend. Remember – it’s free! - join our experts and create your own tech adventure.

Register now for SAP TechEd
Connect with our SAP Champions and Mentors via our directory.
Check out the SAP Champions and Mentors expert session playlists and view their recommendations from the SAP TechEd virtual catalog.
Stay tuned for a blog coming soon on how to prepare for and make the best out of your SAP Teched experience.

Co-authors @Kartik_Dua @KunalBansal @TammyPowlas @gregorw @ankurgodre
